deficits.missing: Formal mathematics -- one of the three named objectives of this guest lecture -- is essentially absent from the book. There is no coverage of interactive theorem proving (Lean/Isabelle/Coq), autoformalization, prover models (AlphaProof, AlphaGeometry, DeepSeek-Prover, Minerva-style math specialists), or the miniF2F/PutnamBench benchmarks. Grep across all content finds only two passing mentions of FrontierMath as a benchmark and one sentence about 'formal mathematics' in 11-evaluation/01-eval-landscape. This is a conceptual gap that matters for the book's own thesis: a proof checker is the limiting case of a verifiable reward (zero false positives), which is exactly why formal math is a frontier RLVR domain.
deficits.fix: Add a section 'Formal Mathematics: Proof Assistants as Perfect Verifiers' to 5.9 (or an aside in 5.10): what a proof assistant (Lean 4) is, the autoformalization problem (natural-language math -> formal statements), how prover models are trained with the type-checker as the reward signal (expert iteration / AlphaProof-style RL), the miniF2F and PutnamBench benchmarks, and why formal verification sidesteps reward hacking entirely. 800-1200 words plus a short Lean proof example would suffice; no full implementation is required for a lecture unit.

deficits.missing: The math benchmark section (MATH, GSM8K, AIME, FrontierMath, OlympiadBench) covers only informal, final-answer benchmarks; formal-proof benchmarks (miniF2F, ProofNet, PutnamBench) where verification is done by a proof checker rather than answer equivalence are not mentioned.
deficits.fix: Add one paragraph to the 'Benchmarks: MATH, GSM8K, AMC/AIME, MMLU-Pro' section contrasting informal answer-checked benchmarks with formally verified ones (miniF2F, PutnamBench), noting that the verifier is the Lean type-checker and that this eliminates the equivalence-checking false-negative problem discussed earlier in the same chapter.

evidence: 5.10 covers the full lecture arc on reasoning models: 'from a three-word prompt trick to Monte Carlo Tree Search over reasoning trajectories, process reward models, and budget-forcing strategies' -- with working code for zero-shot CoT, self-consistency, ToT beam search, a PRM class with tensor shapes ([B,T] -> [B]), and a complete MCTS implementation with the PUCT formula.

5.10 includes a worked binomial computation showing majority vote amplifies strong signals but not weak ones (p=0.6 -> 0.825 at N=16; p=0.3 -> 0.047 at N=64), test-time scaling laws (Snell et al.), and a latency/accuracy resource-accounting example (7B best-of-64 vs 70B greedy).

5.9 covers the R1-Zero phenomenon, verifier construction (math equivalence with sanity checks, sandboxed code execution), the full 'reasoning recipe' checklist, the elicitation-vs-expansion frontier debate (Yue et al. 2025 pass@k critique and the Wen et al. rebuttal), and pointers to veRL/OpenRLHF/TRL/DAPO -- reference-grade coverage of frontier reasoning research.

Frontier research directions are conveyed through 2026 SOTA boxes: 'Active research in 2025-2026 focuses on whether RLVR expands the base model's reasoning frontier or primarily elicits latent capability' (5.9).

Formal math is nearly absent: grep for AlphaProof, theorem proving, Lean, miniF2F, autoformalization, proof assistant, Isabelle, Coq across all 100+ chapters returns only two FrontierMath benchmark mentions (11.4) and one passing phrase 'formal mathematics' (11.1).
11.4 covers math verification (sympy equivalence), MATH/GSM8K/AIME/FrontierMath benchmarks, PRM-vs-ORM trace evaluation, and compute-normalized (test-time-compute-aware) evaluation.
summary: Two of the three lecture themes are covered at reference depth: reasoning models (5.9 RLVR recipe, R1-Zero emergence, verifier engineering; 5.10 CoT -> self-consistency -> ToT -> PRM -> MCTS -> o1/R1 -> budget forcing, all with runnable code and resource accounting) and frontier research directions (SOTA-2026 boxes covering the elicitation-vs-expansion debate, DAPO, async RL). The third theme -- formal mathematics -- is essentially absent: no Lean, theorem proving, autoformalization, or prover models anywhere in the book, only passing benchmark name-drops. Since this unit is a guest lecture with no implementable deliverable, buildable_verdict is vacuously true and the reasoning-model content alone is buildable-grade, but the formal-math objective is unmet, capping the grade at 3.
